FT_IC1.evidence: Abstract explicitly states "data from the ABCD dataset" (Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development), baseline ages 9-10, U.S. multi-site cohort with 9,337 individuals.

FT_IC5.evidence: The PDF is a conference symposium/plenary abstract published in the Biological Psychiatry supplemental abstracts issue (SOBP 2025, vol. 97:S1-73). It is a short abstract of a conference talk, not an original peer-reviewed empirical paper with full methods, results tables, or statistical reporting beyond summary statements.

notes: Conference abstract only (SOBP 2025 plenary/symposium abstract supplement). Study examines social media consumption and white matter FA in ABCD; may have a peer-reviewed full paper elsewhere, but this document itself is non-empirical under FT-IC5.

FT_IC1.evidence: "We leveraged the ABCD (Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development) Study, a population-based consortium study of early adolescents" — ABCD Release 4.0, 22 U.S. sites, baseline ages 9-10.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical archival report using principal component regression predictive modeling and graph theory on ABCD resting-state fMRI data (n=3037 year-2 participants with complete sleep and neuroimaging data).

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed archival report in Biological Psychiatry: Global Open Science (2025). Note acknowledges a bioRxiv preprint exists but this is the published version.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: The paper reports a numeric estimate for 'screen time' as a DV predicted by somatomotor disconnection (beta=0.13, p=2.6x10^-19, Figure 3A). However, the variable label is simply 'screen time' without phone/SM-specific labeling. Step 2: The methods never define the composition of the 'screen time' variable — it is listed alongside neighborhood disadvantage, household income, and parental education as a 'socioenvironmental context' variable, citing Twenge et al. (2017). ABCD's general screen time composite at this release bundles multiple device types (TV, computer, tablet, phone) without a per-device breakout. No phone-specific or SM-specific breakout is reported anywhere in the paper or supplement. This multi-device aggregated composite fails Step 2 (binding clarification: multi-device aggregated time fails IC2 unless a per-device breakout exists).

notes: Primary focus is sleep duration -> somatomotor brain network disconnection. Screen time appears as a secondary contextual variable (DV in exploratory regressions: somatomotor disconnection predicts screen time). The screen time measure is not defined as phone/SM-specific in the methods, consistent with ABCD's multi-device composite. FT-EC2 applies.

FT_IC1.evidence: "Prospectively collected data from the ABCD study were analyzed from 3 time points: baseline (2016-2018, ages 9-10 years), year 1 (2017-2019), and year 2 (2018-2020)." U.S. ABCD Study confirmed.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD data using group-based trajectory modeling and multiple linear regression (n=6554 adolescents) examining social media time trajectories and cognitive performance outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed letter/research correspondence in JAMA (December 2025, Vol. 334, No. 21). No indication this is a duplicate of a prior paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: 'Social media time (hours per day)' is explicitly the IV in all regression analyses, paired with individual-trait cognitive outcomes (NIH Toolbox Cognition Battery subtests and composite). Numeric estimates reported in Table: e.g., high increasing vs. no/very low SM use: PSMT difference -4.51 (95% CI -6.48 to -2.54), PVT difference -3.85 (95% CI -5.34 to -2.37), total composite -1.76 (95% CI -2.72 to -0.81). Social media time is phone/SM-specific (named 'social media time' with hours/day measurement). Cognitive performance (ORRT, PSMT, PVT, composite) qualifies as individual-trait on the other side.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative regression estimates (mean differences with 95% CIs and p-values) reported for SM trajectories vs. cognitive outcomes in the Table. E.g., low increasing SM vs. reference: ORRT -1.39 (95% CI -2.10 to -0.68, p<.001); composite -0.85 (95% CI -1.30 to -0.40, p<.001).

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis confirmed: multiple linear regression with robust standard errors, adjusted models, p-values and 95% CIs reported. All criteria for inferential quantitative analysis met.

FT_EC7_reason: Social media time is the IV (predictor) in all regression analyses predicting cognitive performance. FT-EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis with no SM-as-IV analysis anywhere — the opposite holds here. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Clean INCLUDE. Longitudinal ABCD study (baseline to year 2), social media time (h/d) trajectories as IV, NIH Toolbox cognitive outcomes as DV. Trajectory modeling (group-based) + multiple linear regression. N=6554.
